stories.headline: Downtown Denver Office Market in Distress as Investors Bet on Conversion Boom
stories.summary:
Downtown Denver's commercial real estate market is struggling with some of the country's highest office vacancy rates, flat rents, and dozens of buildings in or facing foreclosure, according to a Colorado Sun analysis. Foot traffic along 16th Street has largely recovered to pre-pandemic levels, but the office sector has not followed suit.

Amid the downturn, developers are eyeing opportunities, including a major office-to-residential conversion project called High Fidelity Plaza. Los Angeles-based The Luzzatto Company purchased two skyscrapers at 17th and California streets for $3.2 million — roughly 3 cents on the dollar compared to their 2008 sale price of $112 million — and plans to convert nearly one million square feet of office space into approximately 700 apartments.

The project will cost about $315 million, with the Downtown Development Authority providing a low-interest $63 million loan to make it financially viable. DDA had already approved similar loans for three other office-to-housing conversion projects, signaling a broader municipal strategy to restore vibrancy to the city center.

The High Fidelity conversion is one of the most ambitious such projects in Denver's history and is seen as a potential catalyst for the broader downtown recovery. The development is located adjacent to a light-rail stop and is planned to include neighborhood amenities such as a bodega, childcare center, and bookstore.

stories.headline: Dark Money Floods Colorado Legislative Primaries, Topping $1.4 Million as June 30 Vote Nears
stories.summary:
Outside spending in Colorado state legislative primary races has already surpassed $1.4 million as of early June and is expected to climb significantly before the June 30 primary, according to the Denver Post. The money flows largely through political action committees that disclose few or no donors, making it difficult for voters to identify who is funding the campaign mailers filling their mailboxes.

The spending represents a continuation of a broader battle between business-aligned groups that prefer moderate Democrats and labor unions backing more progressive candidates. In the 2024 primary cycle, outside groups spent $5 million on similar efforts. This year, a labor coalition had spent more than $570,000, while competing committees on the moderate side had spent nearly double that amount.

Much of the moderate-side funding — nearly $500,000 — comes from a group called 'Fair Economy for Coloradans,' which has no publicly disclosed donors and was created in January by Scott Martinez, Denver's former city attorney. The remainder flows from One Main Street, a recurring financial player in Democratic primaries that favors business-friendly candidates.

Most of the contested races are in safe Democratic districts in and around metro Denver, where the primary winner is heavily favored to win in November. That dynamic has turned some of the state's bluest legislative seats into among the most expensive contests in Colorado.

stories.headline: Jeffco Schools Says 61 Males on Girls' Sports Rosters Were Managers and Mascots, Not Athletes
stories.summary:
Jefferson County Public Schools pushed back this week against a federal finding that it violated Title IX, saying that the 61 males identified on girls' sports rosters by the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights were managers, trainers, or mascots — not competing athletes. The district said the federal office never sought clarification on the roles of those individuals before issuing a press release.

Jeffco stated it has repeatedly asked the Office for Civil Rights to correct the factual error, but the agency has declined. A later version of the federal press release was quietly amended to add the words 'may' and 'up to' when describing the roster positions, but the department has not publicly addressed the change.

The federal office issued a warning letter to the 74,000-student district last week threatening to pull its federal funding if it does not change policies on transgender students related to overnight accommodations, bathroom use, and sports participation. Jeffco maintains it is following Colorado anti-discrimination law, which it says directly contradicts the Trump administration's interpretation of Title IX.

'This places school districts in an impossible position,' the district wrote in a letter to the community. The district noted it is still within the 90-day window for good-faith negotiations, disputing the federal office's characterization that the two sides are at an impasse.

stories.headline: Road Rage Fight on I-70 in Wheat Ridge Caught on Dashcam; Both Drivers Issued Summonses
stories.summary:
Two drivers were caught on video stopping their vehicles in the middle of Interstate 70 in Wheat Ridge this week to physically confront each other, with the incident occurring near the West 32nd Avenue interchange. Dashcam footage from a passing 18-wheeler showed the drivers swerving between lanes and brake-checking one another before both cars came to a full stop in a travel lane.

One driver exited his vehicle and ran back toward the other car, slamming its door shut as the second driver attempted to exit. The second driver later retrieved a tire iron from his trunk. One driver remained on scene to report the incident to police.

Wheat Ridge police posted the video on social media Friday as a public warning about the dangers of stopping on the highway. 'I-70 is dangerous enough without people stopping in the middle of the highway and getting out of their cars,' the department wrote. Both drivers admitted to escalating the situation and were each issued a summons for disorderly conduct.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife Kills Wolf Tied to 10 Livestock Depredation Events in Routt and Rio Blanco Counties
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife announced Saturday that it killed an uncollared gray wolf on June 12 in Routt County that is believed to be responsible for ten confirmed livestock depredation events since the summer of 2025. The wolf had previously been a member of the Copper Creek Pack but separated from the pack in September 2024.

The depredations involved 22 sheep across Rio Blanco and Routt counties, with the two most recent incidents occurring on June 10 and 11 — each involving one sheep killed with what CPW described as clear and convincing evidence of wolf predation. The affected livestock producer had deployed a range of non-lethal deterrents, including range riders, livestock guardian dogs, scare devices, active herder presence, and non-lethal hazing permits.

'The decision to pursue lethal actions is never an easy one but the circumstances around this wolf's repeated depredation history made this a difficult but necessary decision,' said CPW Director Laura Clellan. CPW said a final report on the lethal removal will be posted to its website when complete.

The removal marks one of the most significant actions taken by CPW since Colorado voters approved wolf reintroduction in 2020. The agency said the decision was made after all viable non-lethal options had been exhausted by the producer.

stories.headline: FBI Conducts Simulated Nuclear Detonation Exercise in Colorado Springs
stories.summary:
FBI agents, Department of Energy personnel, and military members gathered in Colorado Springs this week for 'Prominent Hunt,' an annual simulated nuclear detonation exercise designed to prepare responders for a potential terrorist attack involving weapons of mass destruction. The exercise has been held annually since 2012 and is coordinated by the FBI, the only federal agency with jurisdiction to investigate nuclear incidents.

Participants practiced responding to a simulated detonation, including collecting samples from the blast site, properly packaging materials, and transporting them to a laboratory for testing — all while wearing multiple layers of protective gear that was subsequently removed and disposed of through decontamination procedures. The exercise is intended to help agencies understand each other's roles and capabilities.

FBI Special Agent in Charge Amanda Koldjeski said most real-world calls involving radiological concerns turn out to be hoaxes, but stressed that every incident must be treated as genuine until proven otherwise. She noted that calls involving explosives, particularly in rural Colorado where old military ordnance is sometimes discovered on newly purchased property, represent among the more serious recurring cases the agency handles.

In the event of a real mass-casualty nuclear or radiological event, officials said the first public guidance from the National Nuclear Security Administration would be to get indoors and shelter in place until further instructions are provided.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs Approves Data Center Project Despite Community Pushback
stories.summary:
The City of Colorado Springs Planning Department has issued an administrative approval for Project Taurus, a proposed data center to be built on a 21.96-acre industrial site at 1565 High Tech Way near the intersection of Garden of the Gods Road and Centennial Boulevard. The approval was granted Thursday.

The project has been a source of community debate since it was announced in April 2026, with residents raising concerns about its impact on the surrounding area. Now that administrative approval has been issued, members of the public have until 5 p.m. on June 22 to file an appeal request.

City officials said additional details about the project will be released next week, citing significant public interest. The approval allows the applicant to use an existing industrial building and make site improvements to the parcel.

stories.headline: KSE's Josh Kroenke Says Avalanche-Nuggets Practice Facility Is Close, But Fans Have Heard It Before
stories.summary:
Kroenke Sports & Entertainment president Josh Kroenke said this week that a new shared practice facility for the Colorado Avalanche and Denver Nuggets is nearing a final hurdle, telling reporters the organization is working through a pedestrian access issue over Speer Boulevard tied to a master development plan around Ball Arena and the former Elitch Gardens site. Kroenke said he hopes to have more information 'relatively soon' but offered no firm timeline.

The statement drew skepticism from Denver sports media, as Kroenke has made similar promises about new facilities dating back to at least 2017. The Avalanche currently practice at the South Suburban Family Sports Center, a public recreation facility in Centennial that shares space with an arcade and climbing wall — a setup critics have long called unbecoming of a three-time Stanley Cup champion franchise.

The Nuggets' practice situation is similarly modest, with their training space located at floor level within Ball Arena. Columnists noted the contrast between KSE's willingness to spend on player contracts and its long-delayed investment in team infrastructure.

Kroenke said the first phase of the Elitch Gardens master plan would most likely include a combined Avalanche and Nuggets facility. He attributed the delay partly to the complexity of working with the city on development approvals.

stories.headline: Broncos OC Davis Webb Sees Diggs-Allen Parallels in Jaylen Waddle Trade
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos offensive coordinator Davis Webb drew a comparison between the Broncos' newly acquired receiver Jaylen Waddle and the dynamic between Stefon Diggs and Josh Allen during their time together in Buffalo, suggesting he sees a similar potential connection between Waddle and quarterback Bo Nix. Webb's comments came during the team's second week of organized team activities.

The Waddle trade has been one of the offseason's marquee moves for Denver, adding an established receiving threat alongside Nix as the Broncos aim to build on last season's AFC Championship Game appearance. Webb, elevated to primary play-caller this offseason, has been the subject of intense scrutiny as the team transitions its offensive scheme.

The Broncos held their second open OTA practice of the offseason on Thursday, with Webb and other coaches speaking to reporters about the team's development heading into training camp.

stories.headline: Column: Sean Payton Extension Is Modest Compared to the Looming Bo Nix Contract Decision
stories.summary:
Denver Gazette columnist Mark Kiszla argued Friday that the Broncos' new five-year contract extension for head coach Sean Payton, while well-deserved, is financially modest compared to the franchise-defining decision the Walton-Penner ownership group will soon face: whether to commit roughly $55 million per year to quarterback Bo Nix on a long-term extension.

Nix, who guided Denver to a 14-win regular season and the AFC Championship Game last year before losing 10-7 to New England, is approaching the point where a franchise quarterback contract decision can no longer be deferred. Kiszla noted that Payton's path to the Pro Football Hall of Fame likely runs through Nix, pointing out that it has been 16 years since Payton won his only Super Bowl with Drew Brees in New Orleans.

The Broncos this offseason also extended general manager George Paton, traded for receiver Jaylen Waddle, gave cornerback Patrick Surtain II a $5 million annual raise, and are pursuing plans for a new stadium — a run of spending that signals championship ambitions from ownership. Payton said he is 'thankful' the organization wants him back and emphasized alignment between ownership, the front office, and the coaching staff.

stories.headline: Former Colorado Governor Urges Unaffiliated Voters to Participate in June 30 Primary
stories.summary:
In a guest column published by the Colorado Sun, former Colorado Governor Bill Owens joined others in urging the state's large bloc of unaffiliated voters to cast ballots in the June 30 primary election. Unaffiliated voters now comprise more than half of Colorado's electorate but historically participate in primary elections at far lower rates than registered party members.

The column emphasized that Colorado law allows unaffiliated voters to choose either a Democratic or Republican primary ballot without joining a party, and that doing so does not change their registration status. Writers argued that many general elections — particularly in safe partisan districts — are effectively decided in the primary, making primary participation critical for voters who want to shape their representation.

The appeal comes as outside spending in legislative primaries has already topped $1.4 million, with races in safe Democratic metro Denver districts drawing disproportionate attention and funding from competing ideological factions within the party.

stories.headline: Denver Metro to Hit Mid-90s by Midweek After Cool, Stormy Weekend
stories.summary:
An early morning cold front moved through the Denver metro Saturday, bringing cooler and more seasonal temperatures after a stretch of above-average heat. Afternoon highs Saturday were expected to top out in the upper 70s to low 80s, with breezy northeasterly winds and the possibility of smoke drifting in from wildfires burning in Nebraska.

A second cold front arriving Saturday night will reinforce below-normal temperatures Sunday, with highs in the upper 60s to low 70s and a chance of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms Sunday afternoon and evening, making it the coolest day of the weekend. Forecasters described it as the coolest day of the period.

The cool pattern will be short-lived. Warm and dry conditions are expected to return early next week, with highs climbing into the mid-90s by Wednesday. Forecasters said residents watching for triple-digit heat may have a few more weeks of reprieve before temperatures on the Front Range reach 100 degrees.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I think they need a speed between first class and coach. Like a 'pretty good' section.

topStory.headline: Marine F/A-18 Crashes Into Washington State Mountain, Pilot Ejects Safely as Wildfire Erupts
topStory.summary:
A United States Marine Corps F/A-18 Hornet fighter jet crashed into a mountainside near Rimrock Lake in Yakima County, Washington, shortly before noon Saturday, sparking a wildfire and prompting the evacuation of campers in the surrounding area. The pilot ejected from the aircraft before impact and sustained only minor injuries, according to the Yakima County Sheriff's Office, which said a mountain pass deputy made contact with the pilot at the scene. The aviator was subsequently transported to a hospital for treatment.

Marine Aircraft Group 11, 3rd Marine Aircraft Wing, based at Marine Corps Air Station Miramar in San Diego, California, confirmed in a statement that the jet was conducting routine training at the time of the crash. The unit described the incident as a "non-fatal aviation mishap" and said the investigation into the cause could take months. "To preserve the integrity of the investigation, no additional details are available at this time," the statement read.

The crash ignited a brush fire that sent thick columns of black smoke rising above the forested terrain surrounding the lake, a popular outdoor recreation destination. Naches Fire Department and the U.S. Forest Service's Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest responded with helicopters and at least one ground engine to suppress the blaze. Crews also completed structure protection near the Bear Creek cabins before clearing the scene, with a water tender remaining on site and expected to return Sunday for mop-up operations.

Authorities closed the 1200 Road system on the south side of Rimrock Lake and coordinated the evacuation of campers from the area using multiple law enforcement agencies. The area was described as falling within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, approximately 125 miles southeast of Seattle. Officials advised the public to avoid the area for the foreseeable future as fire suppression and crash investigation efforts continue.
